To address the log4j jndi-vulnerability, many companies have removed JndiLookup.class from the artifact, whether or not they upgraded log4j2. This is a safeguard against the potential of newly discovered routes to JndiLookup, which are not yet patched. We would rather remove the JndiLookup alltogether, to take away the core issue: that a logging-api should not allow code-execution, no matter how e
In 2.17.0 the check for the JndiLookup class is based on a FQCN: "org.apache.logging.log4j.core.lookup.JndiLookup"

However, in the master-branch, we see a dependency on: JndiLookup.class.getName(), which in the case of a removed class-file, would fail the loading of *any* interpolator-plugin, as all checks will cause an exception, and Interpolator.handleError to be executed.
`clazz.getName().equals("org.apache.logging.log4j.core.lookup.JndiLookup")`
`clazz.getName().contains(".log4j.") && clazz.getName().endsWith(".JndiLookup")`

I am actually in the process right now of moving the JNDI pieces to their own log4j-jndi module, which is in keeping with the more modular approach we are trying to take with 3.0. log4j2.enableJndiLookup=false
Our app do not need jndi lookup feature, so we want force disable it(Can't enable by -Dlog4j2.enableJndiLookup=true or by ENV)
-        if (JndiManager.isJndiEnabled()) {

debug("${progname} foo ${jndi:ldap://example.com/a} bar");
This change ensures that it remains safe to remove `JndiLookup.class`.

In this scenario, Log4J's `JndiManager#lookup(java.lang.String)` will determine that the object is safe to deserialize based on the value of the `javaClassName` attribute (because `java.lang.String` is on the default allow list), and then proceed to call `javax.naming.Context#lookup(java.lang.String)`, which will deserialize the data in `javaSerializedData`. I refactored and reimplemented using a hopefully more reliable design.  The bulk of the code has been extracted into a helper class and unit tested.  The conditional in JndiManager still remains to be tested.  Thanks for looking into that one, @rgoers.

@vy I don't disagree with you, and if the decision is to remove `log4j2.allowedLdapClasses` entirely, along with all support for JNDI lookups, I'd be all for it.  But it seems unwise to continue supporting and documenting `log4j2.allowedLdapClasses` without a change like this one, given that currently that setting seems to offer no actual protection from an attacker, and so may mislead users who m

Thanks to [LOG4J2-703](https://github.com/apache/logging-log4j2/commit/3203d3eab6bdd12fdad7ded1860db16a89468c3f), I think it's quite safe to remove `org/apache/logging/log4j/core/lookup/JndiLookup.class` from `log4j-core-*.jar` as a workaround. Just `zip -q -d log4j-core-*.jar org/apache/logging/log4j/core/lookup/JndiLookup.class` to disable `${jndi:...}` functionality completely.
I posted all log4j-core jar with `JndiLookup.class` removed at https://github.com/zhangyoufu/log4j2-without-jndi for reference. Simple local test looks promising.

Thank you @zhangyoufu for the suggested workaround for older versions of log4j to remove the `JndiLookup.class` class! The team likes your idea and we will include the workaround you suggested in the release notes and announcement email. Many thanks!
I provide a patch library to solve this vulnerability (disable JNDI lookup): [Glavo/log4j-patch](https://github.com/Glavo/log4j-patch)
It provides an empty `JndiLookup`, and you can disable JNDI lookup by simply attaching it to the front of the classpath.

@remkop , thanks for your reply. Just want to make it more clear, because many people reach this issue mainly for the "JNDI lookup"  CVE, so, for log4j 1.x, although it contains JMS Appender. But if the "JMS Appender" is not used for log4j 1.x (the usage of JMS Appender is rare), then, it  should be not affected by this vulnerable RCE, is this correct?

I guess the RCE can be exploited by using a message which has a JNDI lookup which, is not possible in log4j 1.x as it doesn't support lookups. And JMS Appender can be added to logger either programmatically or from configuration couldn't quite understand how its affected.  If the developer leave it to add the details like JNDI URL (JMS Appender from input, isn't it the issue of application instead

https://github.com/LoliKingdom/NukeJndiLookupFromLog4j is nearly live on CurseForge (modding platform for Minecraft), it'll target any clients/servers running with MinecraftForge below 1.13. It's a soft fix that doesn't hard remove any files. However, there also is a Java App in the repository that allows you to remove JdniLookup classes from jars or zips, similar to @Glavo's deployment.
Does anyone know if removing the `JndiLookup` class is enough?
>Remove the JndiLookup **and JndiManager** classes from the log4j-core jar.
So - is it required to remove the `JndiManager` class as well?

`JndiManager` is used by the other JNDI integration points. `JndiLookup` is what's exploitable in a log message, though if you're using JNDI in your configuration, a man in the middle attacker or an attacker with control over the destination URL can configure the LDAP server to return code that can be exploited.
